summary: The author solved a suffix classification task for student math misconceptions by framing the problem as predicting correct answer suffixes from a set of candidates given a shared context prefix. They leveraged custom attention masks with FlexAttention to efficiently process prefix-shared inputs and trained large language models (Qwen3 and GLM-Z1 series) using full-parameter training enabled by offload_adam. A key methodological insight was replacing unstable single-seed validation with multi-seed ensembles to reliably track progress, ultimately selecting the best models via validation loss rather than MAP@3. Final submissions combined models of different architectures trained on the full dataset with slight data format variations across multiple seeds.
extensive_summary:
## Problem Formulation & Data
The competition was framed as a suffix classification task where models predict the correct answer suffix from a set of candidates given a shared context prefix. The author removed duplicate samples, resulting in 35,960 training instances. Inputs were structured with question text, multiple-choice options, correct answers, misconception candidates, student answers, and student explanations.

## Modeling & Training Strategy
Instead of standard sequence generation, the author extracted last-token features from concatenated `[prefix ++ suffix0, prefix ++ suffix1, ...]` sequences and passed them through a `nn.Linear(hidden_size, 1)` layer to compute logits. To handle the variable number of suffix candidates per question, a custom attention mask was implemented using FlexAttention. Training was conducted with full-parameter updates on a single GPU (A100 80G or RTX Pro 6000 Blackwell) using the offload_adam library to manage memory. Hyperparameters were kept consistent across experiments: 1 epoch, batch size 32, and learning rate 1e-5.

## Validation & Ensembling
A critical discovery was that validation scores fluctuated significantly across different random seeds due to label noise (particularly in the Neither category). To stabilize validation, the author adopted a multi-seed ensemble approach (3 seeds per model) instead of relying on single-seed metrics or multi-fold ensembles. Model selection was guided by validation loss rather than MAP@3. The final submission combined Qwen3-32B and GLM-Z1-32B-0414, each trained on the full dataset with 3 different seeds and slightly varied data formatting. An auxiliary SFT loss experiment using generated justifications from Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking-2507-FP8 was attempted but yielded marginal gains.

## Inference Optimization
Inference faced compute and memory bottlenecks. For compute, the author used LMDeploy's W8A8 INT8 kernel, quantizing models with SmoothQuant (alpha=0.75) to achieve stable 40+ TFLOPS on T4 GPUs. For memory, layer-wise inference was implemented: only 2 transformer layers were kept on the GPU at a time, with execution of the current layer overlapping with disk loading of the next layer. This allowed 32B models to run on a single T4, processing 640 samples per forward pass.
competition_flow: Raw text data was deduplicated and split into 5 stratified folds, then formatted into prefix-shared sequences with custom attention masks to train large language models via full-parameter updates, followed by multi-seed ensembling, W8A8 INT8 quantization, and layer-wise inference to generate the final submission.
data_processing: Dropped duplicate samples to yield 35,960 instances.
Formatted inputs into a structured prompt template containing question text, choices, correct answer, misconception candidates, student answer, and student explanation.
Slightly varied data formatting across training runs.
cv_strategy: 5-fold cross-validation stratified by "Category", combined with multi-seed ensembles for validation stability.
ensembling: Final submission combined Qwen3-32B and GLM-Z1-32B-0414, each trained on the full dataset with 3 different random seeds and slightly varied data formats, with validation stability achieved through multi-seed averaging.
insights: Single-seed validation scores are highly unstable due to label noise and should not be trusted for model selection.
Multi-seed ensembles provide significantly more stable and reliable validation metrics than multi-fold ensembles.
Larger model sizes consistently yield better performance.
Validation loss is a more trustworthy metric for model selection than MAP@3.
critical_findings: Label noise, particularly in the Neither category, causes validation scores to fluctuate wildly across different random seeds.
Multi-seed ensembling outperforms multi-fold ensembling for validation stability.
Storing layer checkpoints in Kaggle's /tmp directory causes crashes due to copy-on-write capacity limits.
what_did_not_work: Storing model layer checkpoints in Kaggle's /tmp directory caused a crash due to storage capacity limits, forcing a rushed submission with fewer models than intended.

other_solution_refs.description: 1st place solution in Feedback Prize - Predicting Effective Arguments
